I've given PS CS4 Extended an overall four star rating. The breakdown is five stars for its capabilities averaged with two stars for the user interface. Most of the low ratings for this student edition have to do with problems getting the software registered, rather than with the product itself. Please read the student license carefully on Adobe's site. As I recall, you have to be an active full-time four year or graduate student at an approved institution that grants four year college degrees. Adobe is very protective of this 199 price. Getting a retailer to ship you this software is just the beginning of the process. You then have to contact Adobe directly for permission to use it. Adobe takes its role as gatekeeper very seriously and will follow up on your student credentials. The burden is on you to prove that you fully qualify as to each and every requirement. I suspect that some of the low ratings come from people who didn't quite meet the requirements for a student license, but thought they would squeak by. Surprise. Be aware that Adobe also markets an academic edition of PS CS4 Extended for 299. I own the academic edition. Other than the price, what are the differences between the student and academic editions? Well, the first difference is that the license for the academic edition is far less restrictive. The second is that the install code comes printed on the inside packaging. You do not need to go through Adobe for any further permission to use the software. My understanding is that the academic edition is available to students at any grade level from 10th grade high school on up, as well as to academic faculty and staff. However, I note that one reputable retailer of academic software states "If you are a K-12 Student, a parent of such a student or a school employee then this version of Adobe Photoshop CS4 Extended is the lowest Academic pricing available." So, it's possible that some retailers are more liberal than others. Remember, the retailer is the only gatekeeper you'll have to deal with because the only code you need comes with the disk. The full price for PS CS4 Extended is over 900. 299 for the academic edition is still a good deal, even if it is 100 more than the cost of the student edition. Many people will qualify who wouldn't have qualified for the student edition. Don't cheat yourself by trying to cheat Adobe out of a student license, or by purchasing from a vendor with a price that's too good to be true. Update 12 8 2009 I would lower my rating to two stars if I could. The problem is that the program has ceased to work since I upgraded to Windows 7 Professional 64 bit. 299 is a lot of money to throw out the window, and Adobe is not being helpful. If interested, please see the review that I posted today under the retail edition of Photoshop CS4 Extended. Update 3 9 2010 The problem noted in my update of 12 8 2009 took me about three weeks to solve. The issue turned out to be very sophisticated. Fortunately, the issue is also very rare and unlikely to affect you. See my review under the retail edition of PS CS4 if interested. To summarize, I received no assistance from Adobe, whose technical support of this product is greatly lacking in relation to the cost. My rating remains at four stars due to the simple fact that there really isn't an alternative to Photoshop. Update 4 12 2010 Today is the so-called "launch date" for Photoshop CS5 Extended. Little new information has been provided except that the actual release to sales will be in mid-May. It appears that Adobe will continue to offer a free trial period beginning on the release to sales date. Anyone who is seriously considering purchase of CS4 should defer to mid-May. The best information I can find states that the current "Student Edition" will become the "Student and Teacher Edition," while the current "Academic Edition" will become the "Institutional Edition." Prices will apparently remain at 199 and 299 respectively, with eligibility requirements similar to the current products. When Adobe released sneak previews of CS5's new content aware fill feature, it was immediately obvious to me that this might finally!! be the solution for the ghosting problem that ruins so many HDR images. Adobe's new preview of automatic ghost removal during HDR merge suggests that its content aware technology is being used for exactly that purpose. Therefore, I suggest holding off on the purchase of Photomatix or FDR Tools until you've had a chance to experiment with the PS CS5 trial. Similarly, I would hold off on the purchase of Mask Pro 4.0 from On One Software. If and it's a very big if CS5 has really leapfrogged over both Photomatix and Mask Pro 4.0 -- both of which I was planning to buy -- then this is going to be a fairly easy upgrade to justify. On the other hand, CS5 could turn out to be no big deal. Be sure to try before you buy.

If you're looking for an inexpensive way to get the power and stability of Linux on your desktop and don't want to spend a lot to get it there, here's all you'll need. Mandrake's Discovery edition is perfect for those who desire a low-cost introduction to Linux. It also gives you installation support from experts in Linux administration so you'll never be alone. That said, there are a few things to look out for in this version. First, if you have Mandrake 9.1 and it works for you, you may want to hold off upgrading as there aren't too many improvements easily noticed by the user--unless you count any eye candy and icon changes. One of the big "gotchas" to look out for is a bug concerning the menus. If you do a clean install of Mandrake 9.2 or even an update , on many systems, after the initial install and after running Mandrake Update, your menu items in the K panel and elsewhere will cease to function and disappear. It was pretty unsettling to me as I did not know how to fix this. The Linux community to the rescue. After you install version 9.2 and run the bug fix update, restart your machine and boot into single-user mode. At the prompt type "update-menus" and your problem will be fixed. All of your menu items will be repopulated never to disappear again. Other than this minor inconvenience, it is a solid version containing the latest version of OpenOffice.org's office suite as well as the latest versions of the many programs and apps that normally come with Mandrake Linux. I was especially impressed with the USB support. I plugged in a USB flash drive and not only did the system recognize and configure the drive, it saved and copied files without incident. No complaints there. Again, if you have version 9.1 and it's stable for you and does all that you need it to do, you may want to hold off until the next version comes out. But if you decide to take the plunge, it is another nice product from the good folks at Mandrake and worthy of your consideration.

Call of Duty 2 COD2 builds on the original. While it is pretty dated, it is a value at less than ten dollars. You can play as the Russians in the battle for Stalingrad, the British from the battle for El Alamein through North Africa and a little around Caen, and the Americans on D-Day at Pointe du Hoc and the Hurtgen Forest. The Pointe du Hoc scenes were especially interesting to me because I actually visited the site on a trip to Normandy. I can say that while the location is not exactly the same as the real battlefield, it was a good representation allowing some flexibility to make the game more entertaining. If you ever find yourself in the area, I would recommend checking out the Ranger Museum in Grancamp-Maisy as well as the actual battlefield site. COD2 has pretty much same controls as the original nbsp Call of Duty nbsp COD1 . The graphics and sound effects were better than any of the other WWII games. You can pick up any weapon in the game. There are lots of options with weapons from each side. In single player as the British, you can control a tank. In another mission with the British, you get to man the main gun of an armored personnel carrier. This game is highly scripted, though less than COD1. There is usually only one avenue to complete missions. Alternate approaches are blocked off with debris or road blocks. There are some missions that have multiple objectives at the same time, and you can choose to assault them in the order you like. I enjoyed the campaign despite the restrictions on movement. Difficulty is variable. I found it challenging, but fun at "hardened" difficulty. The length of the single player campaign was reasonably long, not as short as COD1. Multiplayer is still alive and well with lots of community content. The setup is pretty neat. If you select a server that has modified maps or content, it can be set to be automatically downloaded. However, multiplayer is limited to infantry action. If you like combined arms infantry, armor, naval, and air combat , I would recommend the Battlefield series instead. COD2 has superior graphics to the Battlefield series though. Couple of changes in COD2 versus the original -There are smoke grenades which makes assaulting positions fun. Now if only your AI teammates would combine in your assault and not just wait until you move up to a certain point on the map. -sniper holding their breath to improve accuracy on long shots -Health is handled differently than most other games. No health bar. Basically when you are hit you start seeing red and start losing control of your soldier. If you can get away your health returns automatically. The weakness when you get hit gives an authentic feel to the game however, the automatic healing while it keeps the game moving detracts from immersion. Technical Note The game played great on Windows 7 Intel i 7, 12 GB RAM, 2GB Nvidia Video Card for me. When I was installing the game I got a message saying the game had known compatibility issues with Windows 7 however, I just ran the setup from the disk and applied the 1.03 patch. No issues running the game at highest settings.

After reading the absolutely wonderful reviews across most of Gunnar's products, I decided to give these a shot. I chose these specific glasses because unlike the rest of the Gunnar lineup, the lenses on these didn't have the yellow tint which would have made it a deal breaker as I need color accuracy in my work. First, the overall finish on these is honestly subpar at best. The frame feels like something that you would expect to find on glasses costing a quarter of what these cost. Second, the lenses are absolutely horrendous fingerprint magnets! I can't even begin to describe just how bad it got. But just to put into perspective. I wash my hands, I wash my face, no oil anywhere, everything is clean. I put the glasses on, and within 15 minutes I have to take them off to get rid of a smear from either my eyelashes touching them, or an eyebrow hair falling past them. I honestly spent more time cleaning these than wearing them. But maybe even all that I could tolerate... What ended up being the absolute deal breaker for me and what caused me to return these was that I use 2 displays. A 27" iMac, with an older 20" display next to it. When I tried using these while using a small laptop screen, I did notice they do somewhat help. When I tried them while sitting in front of my usual setup, the 27" iMac screen was scaled up so much that I felt like I was sitting too close to the screen in a theater. The second screen, which stands next to the 27" one, not only kept on making the exposed edges of the lenses give off a weird glare into my eyes. On top of that, what you need to understand is that these are good only for staring into one point without moving at all. If you're used to using multiple screens you know it's easier to just move your eyeballs instead of your whole head. While wearing these if I just looked at the second screen without moving my entire head I was always ending up looking past over the bridge of my nose in the tiny gap left by the frame - and since one eye is looking though a lens and the other isn't, that alone gave me a horrible headache. And to top it all off, my work requires plenty of jumping between the screen and paper - ie look at the screen, look at a notebook on the table. Again going back to just moving my eyes, I kept on glancing under the glasses which resulted in my eyes having to refocus much more than they would have otherwise, again giving me a headache. So to summarize, if your work includes sitting for 8 hours inputting numbers into a spreadsheet without looking away from your screen, and as long as the screen is no more than 20" away, and if it's your only screen, then these may help you. If you work with big and multiple screens and do something other than just staring at your screen without even glancing to the side for a second, then I really really would not be able to recommend these. Shame, was honestly expecting more of these...

I purchased this game on a whim. I saw it sitting on a shelf and thought it would be just another generic shooter with Tom Clancy's name on it that would probably end up collecting dust if I bought it. I couldn't be more wrong! This game is the epitome of addicting fun. It's a tactical strategy game, in the same vein as Final Fantasy Tactics and Advance Wars, with a military theme. You are given a squad of troops, each with different specialties, and you must complete the missions using their strengths to your advantage. The game's plot revolves around a conflict involving the Russians and Ukranians, but really the story isn't the reason you play this game. You can just skip the cutscenes altogether and not miss a beat. The missions, however, are extremely well-done. The game throws a lot of variety your way, and you'll easily find yourself losing hours of your life to this game. The game is also really long, with 35 missions to tackle in the main campaign as well as a meaty selection of skirmish missions. On top of that, there are also multiplayer missions, though sadly the game doesn't support online play. The main missions themselves are pretty long as well, with several taking 30 minutes to an hour to complete...sometimes more on higher difficulties. The 3D effect is pretty neat, though it adds absolutely nothing to the gameplay. It is purely for aesthetic purposes. I personally like to have the 3D turned all the way up, as I love the slick way it makes the game look. This is easily the deepest gameplay experience in the entire 3DS launch library, and definitely worth the forty-dollar price tag!

I've been trying to get all of my stationary internet devices using cable instead of WiFi, so I decided to give this device a try. So far it has worked great. I followed the instructions of making sure WiiConnect24 was off, attached the device, went to the settings, deleted the wireless info and added a wired connection. I was online immediately and have been using it with no problems since. A note for those expecting a speed increase you may not notice any. If you're connected to a wireless G router and have good signal strength at 54mbps, you can real-world get 20mbps assuming you don't have other wireless devices downloading at the same time . However, most internet connections in the US have slower speeds than this, so that's the bottleneck and going wired won't increase your speeds. Plus, you can only download as fast as Nintendo's servers allow, so that may be another bottleneck, especially depending on how much activity they have going on. So this device may or may not help your speed. It all depends on where the slowest link is between the Wii and their network.

I will be writing my review based on the Collector's Edition of the game. The game itself has plenty of reviews out there, by both professional and amateur buyer reviewers, so I would recommend checking those out to see if the game is for you. A quick note, I personally really enjoy the game. It's technical issues are a minor annoyance for me...long load times are easily counteracted by checking my email or the internet on my phone, or taking my dogs out. The game, for me, has been just plain fun. I've found myself enjoying actually just running around exploring the landscape, which is something I don't do all too often in games. The combat mechanic is solid once you recognize that you aren't playing a typical third person shooter Gears of War and as such aren't being expected to "aim" quite as much as you do in other shooters. The platforming is superb...rarely am I missing any jumps because of anything other than my own timing. I will also say that I find the robot companions, for lack of a better word, adorable. I love having them follow me around and engage in combat with me. It's pretty awesome. I guess I've said about all that I need to say about the game...I really like it... ...anyway, on to the review of the Collector's Edition package. First off, this thing arrived in a MONSTER of a box. It was huge. Like, way more huge than I expected. I knew the statue was supposed to be large, but I had no idea that the box was going to be as big as it was. The shipping box was easily as large as the ones that RC trucks that I buy get shipped in...yes, that big. Inside of that box was another cardboard box, which is the external package for the product itself...basically the one that got drop shipped to Amazon. Once inside that, I was looking at the actual box for the Collector's Edition... The box is really well done. The art and design of it is top notch. It matches up with the overall look of all of the other art that has been release for the game. It's very large, though, and will prove to possibly be difficult to display. I have some plans for how I want to display it, though, so I think I'll be able to get that taken care of. Once open, you are immediately welcomed by the steelbook cover and the Corebot Alphabet Decoder. The steelbook cover is great. It's a definite improvement over the "standard" Xbox One game case, and it looks amazing. Again, the artwork on it is very well done. The Corebot Alphabet Decoder is something that is very interesting. It's well made and looks cool. I might try to detail it a bit more with some paint than it is, to make it look more worn and "realistic" than it is. It looks nice, but I feel that it could look a lot nicer with a bit of work. What's interesting about it is it's ability to actually let you decode the words in the game that the Corebots use. I learned this right off the bat by decoding the word "Save" from the symbols on the menu when the game is saving. Their is one Corebot in the game in particular that you actually get audio-logs from. Violet. When you get them, you don't understand what she's saying...and the text is in Corebot Alphabet. I'm guessing you could probably find someone online that has already decoded all of it, but I'm planning on using the decoder to figure out what Violet is actually telling me on my own, because I think it will be an interesting and fun side activity that's not actually IN the game. Cool. After taking a look at those, it was time to pull the main event out of the box. I was impressed with the quality of the Styrofoam. Another reviewer has posted a picture, so be sure to take a look...it's cool because it actually has recore embossed on the side of it, which is unique and interesting in the packaging department. Once the Styrofoam was out of the way, it was time to pull out the star of the show. The statue is magnificent. It's large, solidly made, and weighs a good amount. The detail and paintwork on it are unbelievable. I'm completely in love with the statue, and it will be an amazing addition to the shelf in my media room once I figure out where I want to put it. Overall, I'm really happy with the Collector's Edition. It's been a long time since I purchased a video game edition of this size or price, but I'm 100 glad that I did.

I don't typically review many products on Amazon, but I am compelled to do so to save others from the scam that is No Man's Sky. What sounded like a pretty cool game with nearly infinite worlds to explore turned out to be nothing more than a bunch of bitmaps punched together to make a picture of a universe. The game maker did not lie in that you can travel to all of the planets you see, but the unfortunate part is that once you get to the "new" planet, it's pretty much like every other planet you've been to - same plants, similar animals, same materials to farm that's pretty much all you do in this game , same couple of alien races that somehow populate the entire universe but provide zero interaction other than some dialogue boxes, same exact building structures - you get the point. The storyline is so muddled, it might as well be non-existent. There are no missions or objectives other than to farm materials that allow you to power your ship or fix it when it breaks. Basically, I feel like I was duped out of 60. The developer promised a lot more than I got.

First, I have to say that this is quite easily the best LucasArts game I've encountered, and playing in the Star Wars universe with superb graphics and a wonderful game engine is a joy. A lot of work was clearly put into the visual aspects of the game in particular, and I'd normally give it 5 stars for being one of the best first-person-shooters I've encountered, especially when using lightsabers and various aspects of the Force as a Jedi push, speed, mind-tricks, etc. are taken into account. However, this is easily the most unnecessarily frustrating game I've played to date, and I don't think it was tested thoroughly. The amount of time spent backtracking through areas previously explored to find some box that wasn't shot enough times or some hidden button to press to affect something far later in gameplay is maddening. I can't tell you the number of times I've spent extreme periods of time to "find" something or had to look through a "walkthrough" in order to progress, consistently saying "how the heck was I supposed to figure THAT out". Most games e.g., Return to Castle Wolfenstein or Serious Sam Second Encounter will "shut off" areas no longer relevant, or keep crucial buttons and things somewhat near to where they relate. This one frequently doesn't do that. This game is a must-play, especially if you're a Star Wars fan or someone who enjoys superb graphics work. It's lost two stars in my book for the unusually high frustration factor though, and I'd warn anyone playing it they're VERY likely to need to find an internet game-guide walkthrough unless they like spending hours figuring things out by prolonged trial-and-error. If you have patience to spare, Jedi Outcast is a strong recommendation.

I've been a TurboTax user since I got the 3.5" floppies from Chipsoft I've still got them around somewhere . I'm one of those brand loyal customers until my trust in the brand is misplaced. I've been exclusively desktop CD based given the sensitivity of the financial information - and the cloud is out of my control. I've been using TurboTax Deluxe for years - and this year I have one 1 stock sale - and for lack of e-file on Schedule D, I'm forced to TurboTax Premier? The form may exist somewhere, but I can't e-file? C'mon... I've also potentially got one line on Sched C where my consulting deductions exceed 100. I'm forced for that one line to go up to TurboTax Home and Business or I can't e-file? Really? In the interest of transparency - what are the limitations for efiling with each version? Can they be spelled out somewhere? Not publicizing them is deceptive by omission - "changes have been made" does not cover it. Your vague "call us and we'll help you decide" offer is not transparent and does a disservice to the rest of us making an online purchase decision. Are you afraid that the version specific limitations will scare more customers away then these reviews? We're doing our own taxes - I think we can figure out what product we need if you give us the details what each version will allow for e-filing. It's nowhere on your website or in the product description. It needs to be explicitly stated "Premier required for efiling Schedule D". Not "Premier is the best version for your situation" - Sheesh... I can't take the chance that I'm buying crippled software, nor do I want to pay for features I'll never use. I don't mind paying for value - I'm using Quicken Home and Business - but will rethink that as well. The alternatives are out there. Intuit - you've lost me... I've rarely if ever written a review, but this was too much. For all of us that actually take the time to write something, there are hundreds if not thousands that will stay silent and just move on. Time to be brand-loyal elsewhere...

DA O Awakenings, I just finished last night, and i enjoyed it. First off, Bioware fixed all the horrible glitches that plagued the original DA O. During my play time with Awakenings, my game never froze, forcing me to reload reboot my 360. My party members actually FOUGHT on their own, and did their jobs without me having to micro manage every tiny detail. In DA O I had party members stand around and do NOTHING, no matter how i tweaked their tactics When I first started Awakenings, my main character was walking and fighting in her underwear, the blood dragon armor, and starfang didn't carry over, seems most of the DLC items from the original don't work in Awakenings, and ppl seem to condemn the game just for that minor flaw alone. After you take the Vigil Keep, you get MUCH better armor and weapons. Oghren returns, who was one of my faves and joins the Wardens, he drinks the whole cup of darkspawn blood, LMAO the rest of your party is made up of new members, including Arl Howe's son. YEAAAAH, ok....wtf? The story is very interesting, and gives details on the talking darkspawn, and why there are Blights. You can choose sides, save Amaranthine, or the Keep, help the Architect darkspawn, or kill him. There's a good reason many of the choices in DA O didn't carry over, mainly, you're NOT in Ferelden anymore. Comparing this xpac to a full fledged sequel like ME2 is unfair. Awakenings isn't a squel, is a continuation of the Wardens darkspawn story. Also, Bioware, for any one who bought the game disc, included a strange date on back of the manual- 2-11-2011. DA O 2 perhaps? I'll pre order mine soon as I can. I liked Awakenings, a good meaty xpac, 10-12 hrs, and I have a few other charcaters I'd like to play through. Also, a word of advice if you haven't bought downloaded Awakenings yet, before you tranfser your character over, reequip them, if you're wearing blood dragon armor, and the starfang sword. Those DLC items don't seem to carry over for a lot of ppl, myself included. Unless of course you relish the idea of boxing darkspwan in your underwear. Overall, 8.5 10. Thanks, Bioware, may i have seconds? Thirds?

On my P3 600 384MbRAM computer I had been using NIS 2003 in addition to Spyware blaster, Spybot Search Destroy, and Ad-aware, because I knew from my tech that NIS 2004 or NIS 2005 would bog down that old of a computer to install I didn't have enough resources on such an old computer . So much hassle to keep them all those programs updated scanned, but is what one must do in order to stay protected under such circumstances. Fact was, it was time for a new computer as they do become outdated for current usage as technology advances. When I recently purchased a new computer, it came with a trial of NIS 2005. Of course a P4 3.0 1GbRAM would be able to handle the software it did so beautifully. When the new 2005 NIS Antispyware edition came out recently, my trial was nearly over but I was so thrilled because I had an even better option to protect my computer now, one where I didn't have to worry about being protected from adware spyware anymore. I didn't even think for a moment what software I wanted to go with. With 50 of rebates, it is the cheapest, most efficient solution. I would pay full price though for the comfort I have in Norton. The rebates are a bonus. As a long-time Norton user, I was used to appreciate the pop-up warnings when my computer is under attack. I understand that some are reluctant because of this extra effort, but to me I like knowing I am safe. Like any software, a user needs to read the manual to know how to use it to it's fullest potential. I don't feel it's that difficult personally. I actually had a warning this week that spyware was trying to install on my computer, I was glad to see that warning pop up. I feel so much safer with Norton protecting my computer. I feel Norton keeps on top of things I feel free to use my time for better things when online. I like complete protection all in one software program that updates and scans itself.

Video Games are art. The combination of playability, graphics and sound found in this particular game are perfectly balanced. Graphics The best of all the side-scrolling Castlevanias. Many enemies are large, and each has its own destruction animation. The castle is massive you will travel thru many environments with rich detail and tons of colors. Many of the weapons have unique looks and special effects. Bosses are extra big and nasty. Visual effects are exciting and sometimes startling.. not the least of which is when the castle turns upside down. Sound The soundtrack is a classic. The classical-style pieces evoke many different moods. Instrumentation ranges from violins and upright basses to harpsichords, to full thrash metal band. The sound effects are many and varied. Many creatures scream as they explode melt burn vanish etc. The characters' voices are wonderfully campy. Playability The closest comparison is Super Metroid because you will need to backtrack through areas to find or use a new ability to further explore the castle. This works well because you will slay more fiends, which will raise the protaganist's level. Hidden rooms abound, secret weapons and spells and familiars are everywhere. You can spend 20 hours exploring the castle and still not have covered the 200 and beyond of map. Overall, this is an excellent game. If you have a PS or PS2, try this game out. If you were a fan of Super Metroid, do not finish reading this review- buy it now.

So, originally I was looking at purchasing the Logitech 602 as my wireless mouse due to me liking it so much. I then began reading on alternatives and came across a number of brands such as Razer, Logitech, etc. During my research I came across the HAVIT MS988GT. For the price, I figured why not give it a try. So here are my impressions I first tried this mouse on a game of League of Legends, WoW, and CS S, as well as CS GO. The mouse was responsive and I had no problems with latency or feedback,. I previously used a DeathAdder that was gifted to me previously by a friend. I can say that the mouse is on par with that and I felt no difference in feedback between the two. It glided and responded with no problem. The second game I tested this on was a game of Counterstrike Source and Global Offensive. Again, although I am not a competitive player, I still play enough that the correct hardware may hinder my performance. This mouse stood the test and had almost no visible latency issues during my games today. The feel of the mouse is top notch. It feels almost similar to the Razer Mamba or Type-R. It's a very light mouse too and weighs quite a bit less than the Logitech 602 once the batteries are in. For the price, you can't beat the mouse. Although I do have some criticisms. Things I like The LED's on the mouse are pretty slick looking and give off that "higher" end feel to the mouse. The build quality also makes it seem like it's a pretty solid piece of hardware that'll last for a while. Not too mention the mouse itself is very aesthetically pleasing. The mouse is easy to figure out and doesn't really need much reference to the manual and, as a matter a fact, I have yet to look at the manual. It's extremely responsive and has no visible lag between actions, not too mention the DPI settings are very convenient to have. Some things I don't like Although aesthetic, I haven't quite figured out how to set the color I want without messing with the DPI settings, seeing as how they're the same button. This doesn't really affect performance, my theme is black and blue and the DPI setting I like makes the mouse a pink color. Personally, I like my mouse to be a bit heavier but it didn't take long to get used to this one. For people that like heavier mice, this may not be what you're looking for. The mousewheel, at times, was a bit mediocre on webpages, however, this did not seem to be the case in-game so I feel like this may have just been a one-off issue. In summary You should buy this mouse because it is a solid piece of hardware that has everything you need for gaming at an extremely convenient price. For the 20 price tag, you're buying what essential feels like a mouse that is three times the price. Given the quality of the product and the price point, I feel there was no need for me to look and buy higher priced mice. I can see myself sticking to this mouse for a while and looking at other products this company offers.

It mostly consists of the monotonal narrator writing a tiny bit of code and showing it execute without explaining why things are as they are. It talked about data types in PHP as though booleans, integers, and strings are the only data types. All in all this is truly horrible and I wouldn't recommend it to anyone.

Those new to the Animal Crossing series are in for a treat! This is a game which has few outright goals. There is no princess to save, no relic to obtain. Animal Crossing has no traditional villains but you play the Hero of your own small town... ok... maybe you're the Mayor. Animal Crossing has you play yourself as the Mayor of a small town, but you're not there for the paperwork! Instead, Animal Crossing New Leaf offers adorable animal creatures who mosey along and offer a social setting. There is a residential section where you plant trees, flowers, and bushes, layout new buildings the animals request, catch bugs and fish, and participate in seasonal events such as fireworks displays, birthdays, and countless other celebrations. Animal Crossing New Leaf makes a game out of a social system. Animals will ask you for things in trade, compliment you on your clothing, play hide and go seek, and ask your advice. As the Mayor, much of the town looks up to you! In Main Street, there are many adventurous shops, a place to visit other towns both in dreams and via spot pass, the museum where you store your fish, bugs, art, and fossils you obtain, outfit yourself in the most stylish clothings in fun and fashionable styles or make your own! , and purchase furniture for your home. Your home is customizable with multiple floors and styles to furnish, both on the inside and outside! There is so much more to this game and you are missing out if you own a 3DS and haven't jumped aboard! There's even islands to play on. One for your personal adventures and one for meeting new players from around the world! You're bound to have a great time! If you're not new to Animal Crossing, this is easily the most advanced of the games. There are many changes and most are for the better. Even if you picked up the previous iterations Wii you are bound to have a good time. There is enough different that, for the first time, this title feels like a true sequel to Animal Crossing. Using many aspects of the 3DS, you'll find yourself addicted to this lovable and adorable game. I have purchased it for my girlfriend and myself. We love playing together and visiting each others stores, as well as meeting up with some friends we know across the US and one or two new friends. I highly recommend Animal Crossing New Leaf. It is a definite MUST HAVE for 3DS! -Adam

Update 2015.03.19 Dropping these to a 1 star because the flimsy damn things have now completely broken. I can get by with them by having one side fully extended and the other side pushed in so the band goes over the break. But wow, not only was I underimpressed with the features, but the build quality on this is absolute crap. In looking at the band on the other side I already see stress cracks in the same exact spot that they broke on the other side. Stay away from these. These were headphones I picked up after owning the Logitech G930. The sound in these is far superior, which is what I was hoping for. I also use them to listen to music, not just gaming. Unfortunately the drawbacks on these, for me, are pretty extreme. So this review is going to be biased based on what I was used to from my logitech headset. Range, quite poor. I use these for conference calling as well and would walk around the house, I could get to the far side of the house with the logitechs appx 65 feet I cannot get 15 feet down the hallway without these starting to cut out. Regarding them cutting out, instead of simply losing connection or it becoming fuzzy or digitally distorted, you now have a robot screaming "MIC OFF" in your ear. Even if the Mic wasn't plugged in, it'll keep screaming Mic Off until you get back in range. Had I know there was a robot voice talking to me in this headset I would have never purchased it. Muting. Again another annoyance with the robot in my head. When I mute the headset, it's because I don't want people to hear me talking breathing typing etc. I do not want to miss two seconds of conversation while you completely cut all audio to tell me "Mic Off" If you don't have the mic off though, you get an annoying buzz. According to the turtle beach this is because of the nature of batteries and wireless headsets. Funny how I never heard that noise in my logitechs though. Mic. I like the flexible, removable mic, but the sound on it is far from ideal. I get a lot more breathing sound, no matter how I position it. If I move it to where breathing isn't heard, then I get told I sound like I'm too far away from it. Also, if you unplug it, you still get the annoying feedback unless you actually "Mute" the mic that isn't there. And for the minor annoyance. A flashing blue LED on both sides of the headset. Why I need to look like I'm at a rave, but can't get a small red LED at the end of the mic to tell me its muted, instead of screaming in my ear, baffling. Electrical tape sorted that out quickly. The sound filters, for me, are pointless. I notice little to no difference when toggling through them. Also it tells me what each setting is, maybe it's just doing that as a placebo. Not having any other controls on it, at this price, seems sort of lacking. Maybe that's why the logitech didn't have as deep bass, having more features, but lesser sound. Who knows. The simple play pause of a song video whatever would have been nice. The volume control is interesting as it's entirely disjointed from the system volume. I have a small thumb wheel that raises and lowers the volume. I definitely like this over the big barrel that was very sketchy on the logitech. I can hit the volume up and down on the keyboard all I like though and it doesn't change the volume in the headset. I'm not certain if I like this or not yet. Battery life seems to be good. I can use them all day without having to plug them back in, and when they are plugged in they charge very quickly. So in conclusion, I like these enough that I won't be replacing them soon. I can wear them and they work. They have quite a few flaws and I wouldn't recommend them to anyone based on the current features, but they are tolerable. Hopefully Turtle Beach spends more time on better features and configuration rather than just wasting money on making the headsets look "Dope" and matching the colors of the system you're playing on.

We just purchased this game on Friday, July 4th. Pros Stunning graphics... really top notch! Great voice acting Great story line Interesting use of the hats! Cons Too many "combos" well over 120. Six axis control is not what it's hyped up to be in this game Character control is sub par. Interesting use of the hats! The graphics and colors are second to none! I have never played a video game before that matches this game in use of color! The scenery and settings are gorgeous. The voice acting is perfect! And although I'm only a couple of hours into the game, the story line so far is truly interesting! Now the bad part... To memorize over 120 combinations... which covers all the attacks, defense, stances, etc is more than what many gamers are willing to try to memorize... what works for what, etc!! The six axis controler needs to be refined considerably.. not sure if this is a problem with the engineering of the controller or the game itself, since this is the first game I've played so far that makes use of motion detection. Many times you have to use the cone type hats popular among asian agri-workers as a means of hitting multiple targets great distances apart... by bouncing them off a series of gongs! Case in point, I was in a compound high atop a mountain peak and had to throw a hat probably around half a mile or greater to hit a gong with it bouncing back another half mile or so to hit a gong in the compound where I was at... and sometimes you have to do it in a specific order! You actually spend much more time trying to solve the puzzle of hitting these gongs than you do fighting your enemies! Character control leaves so very much to be desired during the hat throwing. Most times it is difficult to get your heroine positioned properly to throw hats. I DO like the fact that you can do minor mid-course corrections in the trajectory of the hats as it travels out to meet it's target the camera follows behind the hat as it travels across great spaces to the target gong! The camera view while following projectiles is spectacular! I love it! Would I recommend this game to my friends who have the PS3? Yes. Four stars... for both overal and fun... not three.

Calling Harvey Birdman a game is a bit of a stretch. It's more like an interactive episode. If you like the show or sort of off beat humor then you'll probably enjoy it. However, it's really short and you may not want to play it all the way through in one sitting. The mechanics are pretty rudimentary. You get to choose to talk to someone, move to another location, examine some objects in an environment, and sometimes present objects to solve puzzles. When you're in court, you're limited to pressing further on testimony or presenting a piece of evidence to counter a certain witness statement. You can press them for info all you like, but if you present the wrong information too many times you lose a life and eventually lose. However, if you've saved you can get back to the same spot quite easily. The game is quite enjoyable the first time you're listening to all the stuff going on, but after you've heard the exact same lines and watched the exact same videos a couple of times it can get old. If you play all five cases involved, you could probably be done in a day or less, but by the end you'll probably just be getting impatient to find the right matches. The last two cases are not exactly intuitive with the evidence so you'll be doing some trial and error. I liked it, but then I paid less than the cost of a hamburger when I found it on clearance a while back. At it's original cost I can't advise buying it unless you're a really hard core fan and even then it's a wobbler. If you can find it really cheap and you just want something more relaxing and funny to play for a while, then you should get enjoyment out of it.

I never thought taking down the Cartel would be so much fun. I was apprehensive about this game after being totally turned off by my experiences with The Division. I saw another TC game and pretty much noped off the advertisement for it. A few friends picked it up and persuaded me to look into it. Man, I am glad I did. The experience is like an open-world TC game, think Far Cry and Ghost Recon had a child...this is it. You can proceed with just main story missions, or take any number of vehicles and roam the beautiful, expansive countryside of Bolivia. There are numerous amounts of side missions, item finding and just mass terror to inflict on the Cartels. I strongly recommend finding a few friends to play with, as the experience will be greatly increased and fun. Scoping out a base and planning the coming assault, whether stealth is needed or if you want to guns a blazing are all things that you can consider with friends. If it fails, you will get dropped back to the start of your assault to give you the chance to try a different strategy. I am super pleased with this game, and look forward to DLC.

I purchased this software to create stop motion claymation movies. The description on the box states that you can capture images using a digital camera, webcam or DV camcorder then apply small changes and add background images and music to create your own claymation movie. I cannot speak for webcams or DV camcorders but the editing features such as onionskin, rotoscope and chroma key do not work with digital camera images. I wrote Honestech and they confirmed what I had discovered after already opening the box. Nowhere on the box did it indicate that digital camera images were not able to utilize the full features of the software. I was not able to determine the limitations until opening the box and trying it out. Now I am out of the 42.99 I paid for a useless piece of software. The software does nothing more with digital camera images than you can most likely do with software already provided with your PC which is what I had been using Windows movie maker . I am very disappointed please don't get burned like me, don't buy this software. The lowest rating I was able to give this was 1 star, it wouldn't let me put zero stars or I would have.

Yeah, I bought this game on a whim. I had never seen any ads or reviews or anything. All I knew is that it is a stealth RPG game. All the previous reviews will have you believe that this is a terrible game. IMO, they are all wrong. Let's start with the graphics. Are they ridiculous top notch mind blowing graphics? No. Do they look exactly like the same graphics as Borderlands and Borderlands 2? Yessir they do. It's got that same type of animation. No, the voices don't always match the mouth movements perfectly. In Borderlands, they don't even animate the speaking parts! As for gameplay, it has a similar feel to Dishonored but without weapons. You have some pretty decent powers like clouding the mind and enemy distraction. Also, the "force choke" power is pretty cool. Leveling up isn't hard as long as you pay attention and stay stealthy. Overall, I think that if you are a fan of the stealth genre, check this game out. Don't go in expecting game of the year but just have fun with it. I think most people want to dismiss this game as vampire garbage thanks Twilight but it's actually quite good. It may have fit a little better as an arcade game. Oh well.

At least Intuit is consistent. That is, consistent in undershooting the mark. Why, why, why, for example, does Intuit insist on forcing the Brokerage structure on users. The problem with the Brokerage structure is that every active investment from a particular institution is conflated into a single account. These account entries are hard enough to read as it is. It's the equivalent of giving the user data reports only after the reports have been processed through a shredder and then through a shake-and-bake baggie. Another downside to this choice is that if you have several accounts at one institution, you can only download into one of those accounts. Then, of course, you have to go through and delete data from all the other accounts to clean up the one account, AND THEN you have the pleasurable task of manually keying in the data for all the other accounts. And what's the deal with "Market Value"? Seems it should be a fairly straight-forward math problem of shares x current market price. That formula works only if you don't make "Dividend Income" entries with your "Reinvest Dividend" entries. How is a user supposed to make use of the "Dividend Income" report if there is no dividend income entered? And, same as previous incarnations, the support for Quicken for Mac is close to abysmal AGAIN. The whole point of the Mac system is to make the user experience as intuitive as possible. Trying to figure how to make effective use of the Investments component of Quicken is brutal, time-consuming, and most definitely NOT intuitive. On the bright side, including the Investments module in Quicken 2015 for Mac fixes the most glaring inadequacy of Quicken Essentials. It seems to me that Intuit just does not really want to support the Mac product. They'd much rather devote their energies to reaping huge profits from making loans. I can see how they might think that anyone who can effectively manage money, using their software or not, would be least likely to become a debtor. If they're not willing to love this ugly in their minds stepchild, then give the child up for adoption. Perhaps, Apple would be a good home for this sadly neglected child.

Titanfall 2 Official Marauder SRS Stereo Headset Review This headset looks really cool and perfectly falls in line with all other Titanfall products on the market.. Its basic colors of olive drab, black and orange give it the look of a battle worn piece of equipment.. Perfect for the younger crowd.. With that I will pinpoint the fact that this headset must have been designed for small or younger people, they are very small and have little in the way of adjustment to a larger size. The ear cups are very small and do not seal to give that noise cancellation that avid gamers prefer for the best game immersion, my wife who has very small ears cant get a good seal either. All of the controls are on the left cup and very easy to use and consists of a mute button, mic it flips up and down , and volume control as well on off switch.. All controls work well and are easy to access during game play. the directions provided for use and battery install are abysmal, don't look for help installing the batteries cause you won't find it, you have to look very closely and figure it out on your own.. Not a big deal but just aggravating for older eyes like mine. I was very impressed with the sound this headset produces, its crystal clear and in fact some explosions and bombs being detonated were almost startling at times during game play. The mic transmissions are also very clear with no static or volume drop during usage which is very cool. The tv volume can be muted for game play during early or late hours as to not bother other people in the household, the sound will still come through your gaming console and this is a huge plus, it would be nice if there was an external or in line volume control for this application. Lastly I would like to address the comfort level of the headset.. I find myself pulling and tugging at them if game play proceeds longer than an hour or so, again I feel its the small size of the headset that causes discomfort during usage. All in all I believe these to be a solid product as long as your aware of it limitations and most of that is size and sizing of the headset. The sound quality is great and the look is nice. I think its price point is a bit on the high side giving its limitations but still a fun immersive tool for a young or small in stature gamer.

For about the last 4 years I've been using the Nyko play and charge kit with my 360 and I've been very happy with the battery life. My 360 controllers go for weeks on a single charge and I probably average about 20-25 hours a week on the 360. That said, I had high expectations for this kit for the Xbox One and these batteries fell very short. The nicest thing about this kit is the charge indicator on the USB cable. When I got the kit I plugged in battery 1 until it indicated a full charge then I swapped them out and started charging battery 2. I put battery 1 in my controller and it lasted all of about 5 hours. The only reason I gave a 3 star rating is because battery 2 is performing much better and so far has lasted about 20 hours. Other than that, the batteries fit well and the special battery cover required fits as expected. Ratchet is a very fun game which clocks in at about 10-12 hours of play. The weapons are great and vary from energy beam cannons to blade launchers. There are 15 of these diverse weapons, and while all are useful, you will find that you use some a lot more than others. New to the gameplay are "devices", which do anything from absorb health from enemies to stunning them by a discoball, causing them to dance uncontrollably. While these are fun to use, I really only used 2-4 of the 10 regularly. My only complaint would be the less-than-satisfying storyline. I had high hopes, and the story is there, but it really just does not come through like it should. Overall, this a very fun game, and a must-have for any Ratchet and Clank fans, and should be in the library of most ps3 owners. For everyone else, buy Uncharted.

I purchased an item directly from Nova only to find that they charged my credit card a higher amount than I had agreed to when confirming the sale at checkout. The charge they billed my card for was higher than the confirmation email they sent to me following the sale. When I complained about it to Nova, they admitted that their pricing engine had made a mistake, but insisted that there was nothing wrong with them unilaterally charging a higher amount than I had agreed to because the higher price "was the correct amount." The only remedy they offered was to allow me to return the item AT MY EXPENSE and then they would refund the charges minus the original shipping charge. I will never buy another NOVA product, nor will anybody in my household. Deceptive and irresponsible business practices may yield short term gains, but they have long-term consequences. My recommendation buy clipart from the CLICKART company, not from a company like NOVA that contracts for one price, but bills another.

Like a lot of people reviewing this case, I noticed a strong rubbery smell coming from the case as soon as I unpacked it. I let it air out for a few days, which helped to moderately reduce the smell. It's been a few weeks now and the smell has gradually reduced to a tolerable level. I've been keeping my XL and games in the case for most of those weeks and the smell never saturated them. I'm giving this four stars and more importantly, I'm keeping it because it's the perfect 3DS XL case for me outside of the smell. There are no obnoxious labels or insignias the design is very simple and elegant. I can fit two came cards into each slot if I'm careful, making it capable of carrying a total of eight games securely, the hard outer shell is sufficiently protective and the case holds the XL snugly. Everything I want from a case. If strong smells bother you, or you want to use the case right away, this probably isn't going to work for you. If you can wait a few days to air it out and don't mind a faint rubbery smell that will probably never go away, this is an otherwise amazing case. Also, buy direct from eforcity through the Amazon marketplace other sellers seem to be sending cases different from the one pictured here. I ordered from eforcity through Amazon's marketplace and received exactly what I wanted.

A relative introduced me to this game,go about fig and I got to play for a while. She showed me how the locked door sequences worked. So, luckily I had a good ideas on the sequence of events. You go to rooms and finds a series of objects. You can't move on until you find all the objects or the game thinks you have found enough. You are also given 5 hints, which you should use sparingly when finding the objects cause you'll need them for the locked doors. Once you get to a locked door, you must figure out how to connect items numbers images together to open the door. I found that this section was quite difficult, but got slightly easier as I got used to it. Once you are through with this you get to see a bit of the diary to find out what event happened in Ravenhearst. Oh forgot to say you have to put a torn up page together as well. After you get through all the story you have one last difficult "seeking object" and "opening locked door" which is very tricky and no help is given. If you like word finds and that kind of puzzle this is quite fun actually.

This is great software for the beginner or advanced user. I am learning how to work in a digital darkroom. And in reading magazine articles about comparable photo editing products, I was intimidated by the settings needed to make adjustments in the other software. I started out using the 'Smart Photo Fix' under the Adjust menu. This option analyzes the photo and then applies the changes to your photo in a preview window. Under the before and after pictures are the settings that it chose. You are free to make your own adjustments. The choices it makes are right on the money a large percentage of the time. And it makes it easy to learn to adjust photos you can adjust the settings up or down and view the changes in the preview window, you can apply the settings to your photo and then click undo if you change your mind. The software comes with excellent on-line documentation and user manuals. Also, there are user tutorials that show how to use many of the popular features. I've gone through a couple of the tutorials and I have learned a lot. The tutorials are written by Lynda.com - I don't know if the company has created video tutorials on other software but this set of videos are great. While not everyone will need the video tutorials, however, if you do like to learn by watching a video, I highly recommend Paint Shop Pro X2. I recently took some pictures at a wedding and there was some equipment setting on the grass in the photo and was very distracting. In 10 minutes I learned how to remove the offending equipment by cloning the grass and fixed the first photo. I fixed the remaining photos in just a few minutes each. I can't tell where the photo was altered and I did the alteration! I say get it. It is very powerful and easy to learn.

There are some really great things about MP8 that would attract any Mario Party fan or gamer to this series. Toadette, Blooper, and Hammer Bro are all playable characters and it can be a lot of fun to have a little more variety than what is usually offered. The host of the carnival MC Ballyhoo is quite clever and witty and whoever thought of creating this little cool dude, I give major props to. Finally the Carnival Cards you receive after playing a board can be used to purchase all sorts of cool things from more minigames to little statue like objects. Unfortunately the bad things seem to outweigh the good in this game. Except for Koopa and Bowser's board which are my favorites, the other ones seem to be just annoying and not thought up well. In Goomba's and Shy Guy's board the objective and point you are going for never changes, making gameplay dull very quickly and putting the gamer to sleep. The so called "Star Battle Arena" acts as the story mode for the board and it can be very difficult to complete. I probably lost to Dry Bones over 7 times in Boo's board, so frustrating I was ready to throw down my Wiimote and have a child like tantrum. The music in MP8 compared to 9 is horrendous seeing as though for instance when you hear the music from Free Play Arena, it is in your head all day long. If you only want MP8 for the awesome minigames and how well the controls are made out, and character selection by all means buy this game. If you think that this is going to be a game you're going to look back at in 10 years and say "Man this was one cool game". Forget it, you'll be disappointed.

I bought my XBOX 360 Elite from Best Buy. I went into the store, and like other reviewers have noticed, the sticker with the lot was covered up with another sticker. The employee was looking at me funny because I was holding a bunch of the boxes up to the light at different angles, trying to see if I could get a glimpse of the lot . I finally told him what I was looking for, and he said "Oh! All of these have the new processor in them." I was thinking to myself, yeah right. I asked to make sure, "you mean everyone of these has the new Falcon inside them?" and he said yes, all of the boxes with GoBig printed on them are Falcons. And all the boxes there did have the preprinted logo on it. I paid for the system, took it home, and held my breath as I opened the box. I went straight for the power supply, sure enough, 175 watts. I did the flashlight test to be sure, two flat inductors. I bought mine on the 21st of December. All the Premium systems there were Falcons too, the sticker was not covered up, and all of them said 175 Watts on them. I had two concerns, how loud are the fans and the drive going to be? I played Halo 3 that night, and the fans are quiet, A there! The DVD drive? Only slightly noisy, but not bothersome, and the game turned up to only medium volume drowned out the noise. GREAT system, glad I chose it over the PS3 I will admit though, I might pick one of those up too when I have the funds

Halo 4 is a good game, just not the best Halo made. The biggest improvements I've noticed about this game are the graphics and the music sound. In fact, the reason I gave this 4 stars instead of 3 is that I was completely impressed with the musical themes and sounds throughout the game, very realistic and dramatic. In terms of the actual game play, I would say it's overall improved. Some of the things they've added, include a Death cam so you can watch from the opponent's view to how they killed you - the thing I don't like, is that many times their death cam shows them not actually shooting my body, but still killing me... hmm, maybe due to connection issues? I usually just skip it because it's sometimes frustrating. Additionally, the multiplayer is lacking in maps - bring back some the Halo 3 maps, those were the best in my opinion. Overall, for what I paid 39.99 10 Amazon Video credit , I'm happy and content with my purchase. I'm glad I waited just 2-3 weeks before they dropped the price 20 and they added an Amazon credit... if I had paid 59.99, I'm not sure I'd feel the same way about my purchase. If you play the game for multiplayer like I do , you'll find Halo 3 and Halo Reach are probably better and missed. If you play the game for Campaign, you might think Halo 4 is the best yet because of the story and graphic planet views they give you, were pretty neat. Overall, 4 out of 5 stars - hopefully 343 will drastically improve Halo 5, or it might be time to find a new series.

Excellent headset. Pros 1. Sound - Clear with a wide sound stage, catches most of the slightest sounds in games. Not a lot of bass, that's good for me since i was looking for a gaming headset with less bass. It will be correct to call it a stereo headset. 2. Build - Quite sturdy, the body material is plastic but robust. The velvet cloth earpads provide a good cushion for the ears. The biggest plus point is that even though they're over-ear headphones, you ears don't sweat due to the open design. After maybe 2 hours of gaming my ears were just barely sweaty. 3. Cables - They come with braided cable which is always good, it prevents tearing of the cable wire. The cable is removable, there's a separate cable provided for consoles, in case you want to attach it to a PS4 or Xbox One controller and don't want to remove from your PC. 4. Overall great quality headset, also pretty reasonably priced now, i know at the time of release they were quite pricey. Cons My only small gripe is that they could have been made more comfortable with a little lighter foam under the velvet or cloth. Sometimes it feel a bit tight and i have to move it around to make my ears more comfortable, other than that they're pretty comfortable.

Overall, the controller is very light and you can tell it was made a lot cheaper than the original N64 controllers, which is to be expected. And as always, sometimes, it's a pain to get my emulator to detect it, but that's usually the case with all P P emulation controllers. Windows 7 usually does a good job of finding the drivers. However, my main quarrel with this thing since I got it, up until about 5 minutes ago was the joystick. It has a little plastic coating around the shaft of the joystick which, after very little use, will become unaligned and start sticking. This proves to make gameplay very difficult. However, after a little fidgeting, you can crack the little plastic film and pull it out with tweezers. This exposes the joystick's movement mechanism and completely frees it - I can't tell the difference between its movement and the movement of my OEM N64 controller. All in all, it's a good controller for the price, but don't expect a copy of the original N64 controller, either! I would recommend it for people who wish to relive old games and aren't TOO particular about the controller weight.

The wii fit is a very fun game at first, but after a few months it can get kind of monotonous. When you advance from beginner to advance it only adds a few extra steps, nothing extravagent. Some of my favorites are the obsticle course and the kungfu rhythem boxing, but there is only one routine that you do over and over again. Would it really have been hard to mix it up with atleast 3 or more. It also be better if they had more than two levels so that after you pass the first one you don't feel maxed out. I would have liked for the cardio exercises to demand more from you. The running is frustrating because I keep a constant pace but my mii is sprinting and then jogging and back to sprinting again. My sister didn't seem to have a problem with it so maybe it is where I am putting my wii remote. Also I would like the options to do some of the activities for longer periods of time, maybe I want to catch fish for 5 minutes or do kungfu for 10. Finally for the custom workouts, I want to option to choose between any game on the system, not just the yoga and strength excercises. Over all this is a good buy. It is one of the cheepest games, if you don't count the wii board, and you will get your moneys entertainment out of it. Even though it seems like I griped a lot about it, I would buy it again. If you are actually looking for a good yoga routine, I would suggest the Dallas Cowboy Cheerleader yoga video, stretches muscles I never knew I had, its not a wii game just a video.

I loved the first game and found myself addicted to it. This has improved on the first in so many ways. Adding in the ability to be Luigi gave some extra challenge to the game. Super Mario Galaxy 2 provides all of that and more. The only downside I found was that I finished the game faster than the first. I'm not sure why. I think I might have played it more solidly than the first. And I also had played the first game so I knew how to do things. I have to say that the one thing I missed most from the first game was the map. It was easy to use because you could zoom out and see every level. Why are people so negative? I thought this game was pretty good. It's worth the money, it's cheaper than the others consoles', and you get a free movie ticket for Spider-man 3. The new, improved New York is a lot bigger than the previous, and now there's sewers and subways too. The graphics are decent, color and lighting was good, and I haven't had any problems with the cameras, but you have to know that the Wii's graphics won't compare to Xbox 360 or PS3, Wii just doesn't have that kinda of hardware, but it makes up for it with a cheaper price, better than gamecube graphics, awesome contols, and games and characters limited to Nintendo. Anyways, the side missions are of course a little repetitive, but the storys and voice-overs are pretty good. The combat and fighting moves are pretty sweet too, but as you probably know by now the Wii's controls are what make the game so different than the ones before, I really like the way they've incorporated the controls into the game. You can really get into the game, with the flick of a wrist, you can experience the sensation of webslinging through the city, really throw a punch to beat up the thugs and baddies, shoot a web at your enemies and hammer them to the ground, or swing them around knocking over the others. The Wii controls even come in the cinematic parts of the game. I like where they're going with this and I'm looking forward to the new Spider-man game, Spider-man Friend or Foe, coming out this fall. This is one heck of a mouse. Long story short, it is worth it. I am upgrading from some cheap no name mouse and let me tell you, the change was well worth it. The features really are something. I has adjustable weights which i really like since i like my mouse very light but others may like it heavy. The included extra pads are a must have for people who don't use a mouse pad and the little case for the weights are nice since i happen to lose lots of things. After installing the easy to use dvd on my pc I messed around with the colours. It had lots of options and the included profile button is a nice feature in case you like certain configuration for games or multiple users. Bright led's with lots of colour options and breathing effects. I use this mouse all the time while gaming or web browsing. It has a soft matte like shell that will repel more sweat then a gloss mouse which is something I also really like. The mouse is very comfortable, I can easily reach all the buttons comfortably and my hand rests nicely on the mouse and the side buttons. I do not play mmo games so i did not find myself using more than 1-3 side buttons but they where easy to reach and press. The quality is very good. The buttons are solid, well made and not to stiff or lose. The cable is much longer than I would need it to be and since it is a braided cable I would not expect the cable to wear like a plastic one would. For gaming this mouse was great. No angle snapping, no acceleration issues and adjustable settings to fit your needs. In my opinion 16400 dpi is more than enough and around the 2nd dpi setting is where i liked it. Overall I was very pleased with the solid built quality, good software, adjustable weights and comfort. Anyone looking for a mouse that is high quality and works great i would recommend this too. If you want to see a more in depth look and review feel free to check out my youtube video

Garden Warfare 2 at its heart is a shooter, with each plant and zombie having its own unique projectile but it adds some of its tower defense roots to the core concept, making the game quite versatile. Placed around each map are flower pots, where seeds can be planted to create turret, healing, or inhibiting plants. With this, players can establish perimeters and make a stand against opponents, which works especially well in multiplayer but more on that later. Narrative barely exists within Garden Warfare 2. Whatever story there is comes across more like a tutorial at the beginning and comic relief for the remainder of the time. While this isn't a bad idea considering the premise of the game as a whole, it does remove a sense of continuity with it all. At the same time, its hard to imagine a narrative here at all, since plants fighting zombies is rather straightforward. Regardless, the concept works well, but having more content in any game never hurts. The previous point becomes negligible, though, under the notion that the entire game can be played cooperatively. Fellow players can join a friends game and play along with what he or she is doing, whether its Garden Ops, daily quests, or boss events in the open town. To make it even better, GW2 also offers split screen co-op as well. On that note, the entire game can also be played solo, but the repetitive nature of what I would compare to a more subtle MMO premise benefits from having companions. This isn't one of those games where allies are needed in order for it to be fun either, because the game as a whole has something special to it but Garden Warfare does become a grind as the few offline quests give way to the other modes of the game. Garden Ops is GW2s version of Horde mode. Played either solo or with friends, gamers fend off endless waves of enemies from key locations chosen by the player from a set of options. Offering the game this way gives players opportunities to set up defenses based on their skill level as well as how comfortable or familiar they are with each map. The last major aspect of GW2 is arguably the strongest mode in the game multiplayer. The game modes available to play are those expected of contested play Team Vanquish, or GW2s version of team deathmatch Turf Takeover, which is a give-and-take game of claimed territories Gnome Bomb, a mode meant for destroying three of the other teams landmarks Vanquished Confirmed, which any Call of Duty player will recognize and Suburbination, where each team fights to take locations by building and defending either tombstones or gardens. The final mode is Mixed Mode, which has a nice feel to it. Mixed Mode cycles through all the available modes so that the online experience doesnt get too repetitive or bottleneck players into playing only certain classes. On that note, the long range characters have a very distinct advantage in every mode, and they're the most overpowered in the game. While they can be countered by anyone at close range, the targeting system in GW2 is a perfect point-and-click format, where theres no need to compensate for anything except for enemy movement. Even at medium or close range, no-scoping is nothing but the way things work, and they work well. At the same time, each character benefits from the same targeting system, so the only real advantage is how capable one is with any given class though the snipers in this game are easier than easy to master. The game itself is gorgeous too, especially for something with such a comical art style. Details are vivid and movement is silky smooth. GW2s sound effects are on a similar plane comical and vivid. Barbs pop when shot, a cha-ching rings when defeating an enemy, and each character has its own distinct sound. While everything is on the same visual pallet, theres no issue with distinguishing whos who or whats what, even in the heat of combat. The lack of a sprint button caught me off guard at the beginning, but the lack of a quick escape requires a bit more strategy, and the game is better for it overall.

Well first let me start out by saying that this review is on the PS2 version of Alone in the Dark. And from what I'm aware of, is completely different from the PS3 version. Graphic-wise It's the standard average for a PS2 game. Nothing terrible, but nothing spectacular. I'm assuming it would be better on the PS3 or Xbox360, since they have better graphics. Soundtrack This is perhaps the only positive feature this game has. It had some beautifully composed moments in some scenes. Gameplay I found the game anything but entertaining. It felt more like a chore trying to get through levels where most of the time your obstacles are just booby traps or exploring through dark-lit locations. There are monsters here and there but they don't exactly add anything to the game. They felt more like extras. Even the boss fights consist of shooting like crazy until they fall--you'd think there would be a strategy involved since nearly all the levels have strategies you need to develop to get by them. There was nothing unique about the game that I saw made this game Alone in the Dark. It felt like an average PS2 action game you'd pick up on the shelf. Or leave on the shelf for that matter.. Story According to others, the PS3 version of the story plays out very differently. But the PS2 version is anything but entertaining. The game starts off on an interesting note but then the storytelling just goes downhill after that. The characters do nothing to move the story along and were far from interesting and became boring annoying very quickly. The story dragged on for me. The levels and obstacles you had to get through to get answers and continue the story was not worth the time. The story is also very confusing where the ending didn't make much sense. Technical Issues A LOT! When they say "Alone in the Dark" they aren't kidding! The levels are so incredibly darkly-lit. Even when I turned my television brightness up, it hardly made a difference. The game did not have a brightness control either so for the entire game I was running around very much blind. There were places in the game where the sound just dropped a few levels. So I constantly had to turn the volume up. Overall Weighing out everything about this game, it's not worth the time or money. The only reason it's survival-horror is because of how incredibly bad this game was made. My suggestion, avoid the PS2 version of this game at all costs!
